era_context: The early 14th century continues with the Yuan Dynasty in China under Yesün Temür; the Mamluk Sultanate dominates the Eastern Mediterranean; Western Europe is fragmented among feudal monarchies with the Avignon Papacy as a major political actor; the Mali Empire under Mansa Musa is at its height; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia; the Teutonic Order controls the Baltic region.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; reliance on later Arab geographies)
Southeast Asia (sparse European documentation; Chinese sources more reliable)
Oceania and Pacific (virtually no written records)
Eastern Europe and the Steppe (limited Western documentation; Russian chronicles underrepresented)

events.title: Ottoman capture of Bursa; consolidation of Ottoman power in Anatolia
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Osman I or his successor Orhan I, captures Bursa, a major Byzantine city in northwestern Anatolia. Bursa becomes the first major Ottoman capital and a symbol of Ottoman expansion at Byzantine expense. The capture marks a significant step in the Ottoman rise to power and the decline of Byzantine territorial control in Anatolia. The city's conquest is celebrated in Ottoman tradition as a foundational moment in the empire's history.

sources.name: Byzantine sources (Pachymeres, Gregoras), contemporary or near-contemporary

events.certainty_note: The capture of Bursa is traditionally dated to 1326, but some sources place it in 1327 or later. Ottoman chronicles are legendary and were compiled centuries later. Byzantine sources provide some contemporary corroboration but are limited in detail.

events.title: Mansa Musa's return from hajj; Mali's Islamic transformation
events.description: Mansa Musa returns to Mali from his celebrated hajj to Mecca (1324), bringing with him Islamic scholars, architects, and administrators. By 1326, the impact of his pilgrimage is evident in the construction of mosques, the establishment of Islamic schools, and the integration of Islamic law into Mali's governance. Timbuktu and Gao are emerging as major centers of Islamic learning. This period marks Mali's transformation into a major Islamic power in West Africa.
key_figures: Islamic scholars and architects brought from the Hajj

sources.name: Ibn Battuta, Rihla (travel account, written c. 1355 but describing 1324–1354 travels)
Al-Umari, Masalik al-absar (written c. 1340, describing Mali c. 1324–1330)

events.certainty_note: Mali's Islamic institutional development is inferred from later Arab geographies and Ibn Battuta's account (written decades after his visit). Direct contemporary documentation is sparse.

events.title: Yesün Temür's reign in Yuan Dynasty; consolidation of power
events.description: Yesün Temür (also known as Taiding Khan), Khan of the Great Mongols and Emperor of the Yuan Dynasty, consolidates his power following his accession in 1323. His reign is marked by efforts to stabilize the Yuan Dynasty amid internal factional conflicts between Mongol princes and Chinese bureaucratic interests. By 1326, Yesün Temür has established a degree of stability, though the underlying tensions that will lead to his death in 1328 remain unresolved.

sources.name: Yuanshi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty), compiled 14th–15th century
Chinese historiographic consensus on Yuan Dynasty

events.certainty_note: Yesün Temür's reign and the internal conflicts of the Yuan Dynasty are well-attested in official histories, though these were compiled after the dynasty's fall.

events.title: Muhammad an-Nasir Muhammad's reign in Mamluk Sultanate
events.description: Muhammad an-Nasir Muhammad (r. 1310–1341, with interruptions) continues his reign as Mamluk Sultan of Egypt and the Levant. By 1326, he has consolidated his power and eliminated major rivals. His reign is marked by economic prosperity, the expansion of Cairo, and the strengthening of Islamic institutions. The Mamluk Sultanate under his rule becomes the dominant power in the Eastern Mediterranean and a major patron of Islamic culture.

sources.name: Al-Maqrizi, Kitab al-Suluk (history of the Mamluks, written early 15th century)
Ibn Taghribirdi, al-Nujum al-Zahira (history of the Mamluks, written 15th century)

events.certainty_note: Muhammad an-Nasir Muhammad's reign is attested in later Mamluk chronicles. Contemporary documentation is limited; most sources were compiled centuries later.

events.title: Charles IV of France's reign; feudal conflicts and papal relations
events.description: Charles IV (r. 1322–1328), the last direct Capetian king of France, continues his reign amid feudal conflicts and complex relations with the Avignon Papacy. By 1326, Charles IV is aging and has no surviving male heir, setting the stage for the succession crisis that will follow his death in 1328. His reign is marked by efforts to consolidate royal authority and manage the competing interests of the French nobility.

sources.name: French royal records and parliamentary rolls

events.certainty_note: Charles IV's reign and the succession crisis are well-documented in contemporary French records and later chronicles.
cross_references: 1328-002 (Philip VI's accession following Charles IV's death)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Ottoman capture of Bursa is disputed. While 1326 is commonly cited, some sources place it in 1327 or later (as late as 1331). Ottoman chronicles are legendary and unreliable for precise dating. Additionally, the 'capture' of Bursa is sometimes attributed to Osman I, but modern scholarship increasingly credits Orhan I with this conquest. The significance of Bursa's capture is sometimes overstated as a 'turning point' in Ottoman history; the empire's true consolidation and expansion occurred over decades under Orhan I and his successors. Regarding Yesün Temür's 'consolidation' of power, modern scholarship emphasizes the Yuan Dynasty's structural decline and the growing autonomy of regional warlords, particularly in southern China. The notion of 'stability' under Yesün Temür is therefore misleading.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1326 is uneven across regions. European sources (French royal records, papal documents) are relatively rich and contemporary. Chinese sources (Yuan official histories) are substantial but were compiled by the succeeding Ming Dynasty and reflect Ming biases. Ottoman sources are legendary and were compiled centuries later; Byzantine sources provide some contemporary corroboration but are limited in scope. West African sources are sparse and mediated through later Arab geographies. The historical record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Court-sponsored narratives dominate; the perspectives of non-elite populations are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: Ottoman expansion continues under Osman I
Mali's Islamic institutional development follows Mansa Musa's hajj in 1324
Yesün Temür's reign continues from his accession in 1323
Muhammad an-Nasir Muhammad's reign continues from his first accession in 1310
Charles IV's reign continues from his accession in 1322
